elements.sent-2411-001: Out-of-distribution (OOD) detection is crucial for ensuring reliable deployment of machine learning models.
elements.sent-2411-002: Recent advancements focus on utilizing easily accessible auxiliary outliers (e.g., data from the web or other datasets) in training.
elements.sent-2411-003: However, we experimentally reveal that these methods still struggle to generalize their detection capabilities to unknown OOD data, due to the limited diversity of the auxiliary outliers collected.
elements.sent-2411-004: Therefore, we thoroughly examine this problem from the generalization perspective and demonstrate that a more diverse set of auxiliary outliers is essential for enhancing the detection capabilities.
elements.sent-2411-005: However, in practice, it is difficult and costly to collect sufficiently diverse auxiliary outlier data.
elements.sent-2411-006: Therefore, we propose a simple yet practical approach with a theoretical guarantee, termed Diversity-induced Mixup for OOD detection (diverseMix), which enhances the diversity of auxiliary outlier set for training in an efficient way.
elements.sent-2411-007: Extensive experiments show that diverseMix achieves superior performance on commonly used and recent challenging large-scale benchmarks, which further confirm the importance of the diversity of auxiliary outliers.
elements.sent-2411-009: The OOD problem occurs when machine learning models encounter data that differs from the distribution of training data.
elements.sent-2411-010: In such scenarios, models may make incorrect predictions, leading to safety-critical issues in real-world applications, e.g., autonomous driving~\citep{drive and medical diagnosis~\citep{Liang2022 .}}
elements.sent-2411-011: To ensure the reliability of the outputs of model, it is essential not only to achieve good performance on in-distribution (ID) samples, but also to detect potential OOD samples, thus avoiding making erroneous decisions in test.
elements.sent-2411-012: Therefore, OOD detection has become a critical challenge for the secure deployment of machine learning models \citep{safety1, safety2, Li2022, Liu2023 .}
elements.sent-2411-013: Several significant studies \citep{MSP,mahala,ODIN focus on detecting OOD examples using only ID data in training.}
elements.sent-2411-014: However, due to a lack of supervision information from unknown OOD data, it is difficult for these methods to achieve satisfactory performance in detecting OOD samples.
elements.sent-2411-015: Recent methods \citep{oe,energy,ATOM,POEM involve training model with easily available auxiliary outliers (e.g., data from the web or other datasets), with the hope that the detection ability can generalize to unknown OOD.}
elements.sent-2411-016: However, as shown in Fig.~\ref{fig:1 (a)-(b), we experimentally find that while the use of outlier datasets can enhance performance in OOD detection, the generalization capabilities of these methods remain significantly limited.}
elements.sent-2411-017: Specifically, there is a considerable risk of the model overfitting to the auxiliary outliers, consequently failing to identify OOD samples that deviate markedly.
elements.sent-2411-018: The above limitation motivates the following important yet under-explored question: What are the theoretical principles underlying these methods that enable better utilization of outliers?.
elements.figure-2411-001: \includegraphics[width=0.9\textwidth]{pic/fig1_new.pdf} \caption{ OOD score for different training strategies. The ID data $\mathcal{X}_{in}\subset \mathbb{R}^2$ is sampled from three distinct Gaussian distributions, each representing a different class. The auxiliary outliers are sampled from a Gaussian mixture model away from the ID data, where the number of mixture components indicates the number of classes contained in auxiliary outliers. (a) The model trained without auxiliary outliers fails to detect OOD. (b) Incorporating a less diverse set of auxiliary outliers (10 classes) during training enables partial OOD detection, but overfits auxiliary outliers. (c) OOD detection is improved with a more diverse set of auxiliary outliers (1000 classes). (d) diverseMix enriches the diversity of outliers (10 classes) through creating significantly distinct mixed outliers. } \label{fig:1}
elements.sent-2411-019: In this work, we theoretically investigate this crucial question from the perspective of generalization ability~\citep{rademacher .}
elements.sent-2411-020: Specifically, we first conduct a theoretical analysis to demonstrate how the distribution shift between auxiliary outlier training set and test OOD data affects the generalization capability of OOD detector.
elements.sent-2411-021: Accordingly, a generalization bound is induced on the test-time OOD detection error of classifier, considering both empirical error and the error caused by the distribution shift between test OOD data and auxiliary outliers.
elements.sent-2411-022: Based on the theory, we deduce an intuitive conclusion that a more diverse set of auxiliary outliers can reduce the distribution shift error and effectively lower the upper bound of the OOD detection error.
elements.sent-2411-023: As shown in Figure~\ref{fig:1 (b)-(c), the model trained with a more diverse set of auxiliary outliers achieves better OOD detection.}
elements.sent-2411-024: However, in practice, it is difficult and costly to collect sufficiently diverse outlier data.
elements.sent-2411-025: Therefore, a natural question arises - how to guarantee the effective utilization of a fixed set of auxiliary outliers? Inspired from the theoretical principles, we propose a simple yet effective method called Diversity-induced Mixup (diverseMix) for OOD detection, which introduces and improves the mixup strategy to enhance the outlier diversity.
elements.sent-2411-026: Specifically, diverseMix employs semantic-level interpolation to generate mixed samples, creating new outliers that significantly deviate from their original counterparts.
elements.sent-2411-027: Given the risk that a random interpolation strategy (merely sampling from a predefined prior distribution) might produce mixed outliers that are unhelpful for the model (as the model can already detect them effectively), diverseMix dynamically adjusts its interpolation strategy based on original samples.
elements.sent-2411-028: This adjustment ensures that the generated outliers are novel and distinct from those previously encountered by the model, thereby enhancing diversity throughout the training process.
elements.sent-2411-029: As shown in Figure~\ref{fig:1 (b)-(d), diverseMix effectively boosts the diversity of auxiliary outliers, leading to improved OOD detection performance.}
elements.sent-2411-030: The contributions of this paper are summarized as follows:.
elements.sent-2411-031: We provide a theoretical analysis of the generalization error linked to methods trained with auxiliary outliers.
elements.sent-2411-032: By establishing an upper bound for expected error, we reveal the connection between auxiliary outlier diversity and the upper bound of OOD detection error.
elements.sent-2411-033: Our theoretical insights emphasize the importance of leveraging diverse auxiliary outliers to enhance the generalization capacity of the OOD detector.
elements.sent-2411-034: Constrained by the prohibitive cost of collecting outliers with sufficient diversity, we propose the Diversity-induced Mixup (diverseMix) for OOD detection, a simple yet effective strategy which is theoretically guaranteed to improve OOD detection performance.
elements.sent-2411-035: The proposed diverseMix achieves state-of-the-art OOD detection performance, outperforming existing methods on both standard and recent large-scale benchmarks.
elements.sent-2411-036: Remarkably, our method exhibits significant improvements over advanced methods, showing relative performance improvements of $24.4\%$ and $43.8\%$ (in terms of FPR95) on the CIFAR-10 and CIFAR-100 datasets, respectively.
elements.sent-2411-037: We provide a brief review of prior research relevant to our work followed by a comparison.. Auxiliary-Outlier-Free OOD Detection.
elements.sent-2411-038: One early work by~\citep{MSP pioneered the field of OOD detection, introducing a baseline method based on maximum softmax probability.}
elements.sent-2411-039: However, it has since been established, as noted by \citep{NMSP , that this approach is not quite suitable for OOD detection.}
elements.sent-2411-040: To address this, various methods have been developed that operate in the logit space to enhance OOD detection.
elements.sent-2411-041: These include ODIN \citep{ODIN , energy score \citep{energy, energy1, energy2 , ReAct \citep{react , logit normalization \citep{logit_Normalization , Mahalanobis distance \citep{mahala , and KNN-based scoring \citep{KNN .}}}}}}
elements.sent-2411-042: However, post-hoc OOD detection methods that do not involve pre-training on a substantial dataset generally exhibit poorer performance compared to methods that leverage auxiliary datasets for model regularization ~\citep{npretrain .}
elements.sent-2411-044: Recent advancements in OOD detection have focused on incorporating easily available auxiliary outliers into the model regularization process.
elements.sent-2411-045: Outlier exposure \citep{oe encourage models to predict uniform distributions for outliers, and Energy-based learning \citep{energy widens the energy gap between ID and OOD distribution.}}
elements.sent-2411-046: However, performance heavily depends on outlier quality.
elements.sent-2411-047: ATOM \citep{ATOM , POEM \citep{POEM , and DOS \citep{dos enhance performance by improving the sampling strategy for auxiliary outliers.}}}
elements.sent-2411-048: DivOE \citep{divoe and DAL \citep{DAL improve outlier quality in a learnable manner, either in the sample space or feature space, respectively.}}
elements.sent-2411-049: Additionally, DOE implicitly enhances outlier informativeness through model perturbation.
elements.sent-2411-050: Incorporating outliers during training often achiveves superior performance, as shown in many other works \citep{energy,SSD,ID-like,logit_Normalization .}
elements.sent-2411-052: Several existing methods have explored the utilization of mixup in OOD detection.
elements.sent-2411-053: MixOE \citep{mixoe and OpenMix \citep{openmix perform mixup between ID data and outliers, linearly representing the transition from ID to OOD and thus enhancing the model capturing the uncertainty from outliers.}}
elements.sent-2411-054: Meanwhile, MixOOD \citep{mixOOD employ mixup on ID data to generate outliers for training.}
elements.sent-2411-055: Different from existing research which primarily focuses on refining mixup strategy or designing outlier regularization method, we place emphasis on the theoretical significance of auxiliary outlier diversity.
elements.sent-2411-056: Our approach advances this concept by enhancing outlier diversity via mixup based strategy, guaranteed by a robust theoretical framework.
elements.sent-2411-057: This focus on enhancing the diversity of auxiliary outliers distinguishes our research from prevailing studies in this area.
elements.section-2411-003: Theory: Diverse Auxiliary Outliers Boost OOD Detection

elements.sent-2411-059: We begin by introducing the key notations for OOD detection in Sec.~\ref{preliminaries .}
elements.sent-2411-060: In Sec.~\ref{bound , we establish a generalization bound which highlights the critical role for auxiliary outliers in influencing the generalization capacity of OOD detection methods.}
elements.sent-2411-061: Finally, in Sec.~\ref{diversity , we demonstrate how a diverse set of auxiliary outliers effectively mitigate the distribution shift errors, consequently lowering the upper bound of error.}

elements.subsection-2411-004: Theoretical Insights: Semantic Interpolation Guarantees Enhanced Diversity of Outliers
elements.sent-2411-099: Mixup \citep{mixup is a widely used machine learning technique to augment training data by creating synthetic samples, which has been extensively utilized in various studies\citep{Umix,Remix,Cmix .}}
elements.sent-2411-100: It involves generating virtual training samples (referred to as mixed samples) through linear interpolations between data points and corresponding labels, given by: \begin{equation}\hat{x} = \lambda x_i + (1-\lambda)x_j,\quad\hat{y} = \lambda y_i + (1-\lambda)y_j,\end{equation} where $(x_i, y_i)$ and $(x_j, y_j)$ are two samples drawn randomly from the empirical training distribution, and $\lambda \in [0,1]$ is usually sampled from a Beta distribution with parameter $\alpha$ denoted as $Beta(\alpha,\alpha)$.
elements.sent-2411-101: This technique assumes a linear relationship between semantics (labels) and features (in data), allowing us to create new mixed samples that deviate significantly from the semantics of the original ones by combining features from samples with distinct semantics.
elements.sent-2411-102: These new mixed samples are situated outside of the original data manifold \citep{manifold .}
elements.sent-2411-103: We summarize this assumption as follows: \begin{assumption (Semantic Change under Mixup).}
elements.sent-2411-104: Let $x_i$ and $x_j$ be any two data points from input spaces $\mathcal{X ^{y_i}$ and $\mathcal{X ^{y_j}$, respectively, where $y_i$ and $y_j$ are corresponding semantic labels and $y_i \neq y_j$.}}
elements.sent-2411-105: If $ \zeta < \lambda < 1-\zeta $, then there exists a positive value $\zeta$ such that the mixed data point $\hat{x = \lambda x_i + (1-\lambda)x_j$ does not belong to either $\mathcal{X ^{y_i}$ or $\mathcal{X ^{y_j}$.}}}
elements.sent-2411-106: Specifically, applying mixup to outliers in $\mathcal{X _{aux}$ results in some generated mixed outliers having different semantics, suggesting that they belong to novel (unknown or unnamed) semantic classes outside of $\mathcal{X _{aux}$.}}
elements.sent-2411-107: Consequently, these mixed outliers can be considered as samples from a broader region within the input space.
elements.sent-2411-108: As per Definition~\ref{def_diversity , the mixed outliers exhibit greater diversity than the original outliers.}

elements.sent-2411-118: Next, we will provide an implementation of our method which dynamically adjusts the interpolation strategy in a data-adaptive manner.
elements.sent-2411-119: Considering a classifier network $\theta$ and $F(x,\theta)$ denotes the logit outputs for input $x$, our goal is to use the scoring function $S(x,\theta)$ to develop an OOD detector: \begin{equation}\label{eq1} G(x) = \text{ID} \cdot \mathbf{1}\{ S(x, \theta) \geq \gamma \} + \text{OOD} \cdot \mathbf{1}\{ S(x, \theta) < \gamma \},\end{equation} where $\mathbf{1}\{\cdot\}$ is the indicator function, $\gamma$ is the threshold, typically chosen to ensure that a significant proportion (e.g., 95\%) of ID data is accurately identified.
elements.sent-2411-120: The training objective is given by: \begin{eqnarray}\label{eq3} \begin{aligned} \arg\min\limits_{\theta} \ \mathbb{E}_{(x,y)\sim \mathcal{D}_{id}}[\mathcal{L}_{\text{CE}}(F(x,\theta),y)] +\omega \cdot \mathcal{L}_{\text{aux}}, \end{aligned}\end{eqnarray} where $\mathcal{L _{\text{CE (\cdot)$ is the cross entropy loss, $\mathcal{L _{\text{aux }$ serves as a regularization term enabling model to learn from auxiliary outliers with low-confidence predictions, and $\omega$ controls the strength of regularization.}}}}}
elements.sent-2411-121: Our previous analysis showed that semantic interpolation can increase the diversity of outliers, thereby enhancing the model's OOD detection performance.
elements.sent-2411-122: However, the interpolation weights in vanilla mixup is randomly sampled from a preset prior distribution (e.g. beta distribution), which may result in generating mixed outliers that are not necessarily beneficial to the model.
elements.sent-2411-123: To efficiently increase the diversity of auxiliary outliers, we dynamically adjust the mixup strategy based on the original outliers, thereby generating novel mixed outliers which are more likely to be unfamiliar to the model.
elements.sent-2411-124: During each training epoch, outliers are regularized, prompting the model $\theta$ to assign lower scores to previously encountered outliers.
elements.sent-2411-125: Consequently, outliers that achieve higher scores $S(x,\theta)$ are more likely to be novel or previously unseen outliers.
elements.sent-2411-126: We expect the generated outliers to be located in the vicinal space of the novel outliers that have not yet been encountered by the model.
elements.sent-2411-127: To achieve this, we adjust the prior distribution based on scores.
elements.sent-2411-128: Specifically, for outlier samples $x_i$ and $x_j$ randomly drawn from the empirical auxiliary outlier distribution, the mixed outliers are formulated as follows: \begin{eqnarray}\label{diverseMix} \hat{x} = \lambda x_i + (1-\lambda)x_j, \ \lambda \sim \text{Beta}(\hat{s}_i \alpha,\hat{s}_{j} \alpha),\end{eqnarray} where $\hat{s _i$,$\hat{s _j$ adjusts the original Beta distribution according to $x_i$ and $x_j$, which is defined as follows: \begin{eqnarray}\label{adaptive_method} \hat{s}_i =\frac{\exp(S(x_i, \theta)/T)}{\sum_{k \in \{i, j\}} \exp(S(x_k, \theta)/T)},\end{eqnarray} with $T$ representing the temperature parameter.}}
elements.sent-2411-129: This adaptive strategy assigns higher weights to the outliers that contain more information unknown to the current model, ensuring the generation of novel outliers, thereby increasing diversity throughout the training process.
elements.sent-2411-130: After constructing the mixed auxiliary outliers, they are used for the training objective (\ref{eq3 ).}

Output: model parameters $\theta$. \For{each iteration}{ \For{each mini-batch}{ Sample $N$ ID data from $\mathcal{D}_{\text{id}}$ as $\mathcal{B}_{\text{id}}$ and $N$ outliers from $\mathcal{D}_{\text{aux}}$ as $\mathcal{B}_{\text{aux}}$, respectively. Evaluate the auxiliary outliers $\mathcal{B}_{\text{aux}}$ using the current model $\theta$ to obtain the scores $\mathcal{S}$. Randomly shuffle $\mathcal{B}_{\text{aux}}$ and the corresponding scores $\mathcal{S}$ to generate $\mathcal{B}_{\text{aux}}'$ and $\mathcal{S}'$. Generate prior adjustment strategies based on scores $\mathcal{S}$ and $\mathcal{S}'$ according to Eq.~\ref{adaptive_method}. Sample the interpolation weight from the adjusted prior distribution and generate mixed outliers $\mathcal{D}_{mix}$ according to Eq.~\ref{diverseMix}. Train the model $\theta$ using the objective function defined in Eq.~\ref{eq3}. } end for } end for

elements.sent-2411-156: To evaluate the effectiveness of our method, we conduct experiments on the ImageNet benchmark.
elements.sent-2411-157: Following\citep{openood15 , We categorize the OOD test set into two distinct groups: near-OOD and far-OOD.}
elements.sent-2411-158: For each group, we report the average performance metrics.
elements.sent-2411-159: Furthermore, we also present the overall average performance on the OOD test sets.
elements.sent-2411-160: Table~\ref{large_scale illustrates that methods requiring outliers during training tend to excel in near OOD detection but fall short in far-OOD detection, sometimes even performing worse than methods that do not require outliers during training.}
elements.sent-2411-161: Although MixOE improves far-OOD detection performance to some extent, it fails to fully leverage auxiliary outliers to enhance near-OOD detection.
elements.sent-2411-162: In contrast, our method not only maintains strong performance in near-OOD detection but also significantly improves performance in far-OOD scenarios.
elements.sent-2411-163: We speculate that the virtual auxiliary outlier data generated by diverseMix may be more representative of far-OOD data.
elements.sent-2411-164: While most OOD detection methods face difficulties in achieving satisfactory performance across both near-OOD and far-OOD, our method excels in detecting both types of OOD, significantly surpassing other methods in the average OOD detection performance.

elements.sent-2411-168: These findings not only suggest the versatility of our method but also provide substantial empirical evidence supporting our theoretical framework.

elements.sent-2411-171: Our method diverseMix consistently outperforms previous methods by enhancing the diversity of auxiliary outliers across different dataset sizes and diversity levels.
elements.sent-2411-172: This suggests that diverseMix remains effective even when the auxiliary outliers are of low quality.
elements.sent-2411-173: Sample adaptive semantic interpolation contributing to unique advantages of diverseMix (Q5).
elements.sent-2411-174: We compared diverseMix with other data augmentation methods.
elements.sent-2411-175: As shown in Table~\ref{ablation (a), diverseMix demonstrates superior performance for OOD detection over other data augmentation methods that preserve the semantics of outliers.}
elements.sent-2411-176: Additionally, the ablation study in Table~\ref{ablation (b) compares diverseMix with different mixup strategies.}
elements.sent-2411-177: DiverseMix outperforms both vanilla mixup and cutmix by adaptively adjusting its interpolation strategy based on the given outliers, thereby efficiently generating novel mixed outlier samples to enhance diversity.
elements.sent-2411-178: The advantages of diverseMix lie in 1) enhancing the diversity of outliers at the semantic level, and 2) efficiently boosting diversity by adaptively adjusting its strategy for the given outlier samples.

elements.sent-2411-189: In this study, we demonstrate that the performance of OOD detection methods is hindered by the distribution shift between unknown test OOD data and auxiliary outliers.
elements.sent-2411-190: Through rigorous theoretical analysis, we demonstrate that enhancing the diversity of auxiliary outliers can effectively mitigate this problem.
elements.sent-2411-191: Constrained by limited access to auxiliary outliers and the high cost of data collection, we introduce diverseMix, an effective method that enhances the diversity of auxiliary outliers and significantly improves model performance.
elements.sent-2411-192: The effectiveness of diverseMix is supported by both theoretical analysis and empirical evidence.
elements.sent-2411-193: Furthermore, our theory enables future research to design new OOD detection method.
elements.sent-2411-194: We hope that our research can bring more attention to the diversity in OOD detection.

elements.sent-2411-205: In this section, we analyze the generalization error of the OOD detector training with auxiliary outliers.
elements.sent-2411-206: First, we recall the setting from Sec.~\ref{preliminaries , our goal is to train a detector with auxiliary outliers that can perform well on real-world OOD data.}

elements.sent-2411-208: Next, we develop bounds on the OOD detection performance of a detector training with auxiliary outliers, which can be formulated as follow: (Generalization Bound of OOD Detector).

elements.sent-2411-291: In this experiment, we aim to explore the effect of outlier quality on OOD detection performance.
elements.sent-2411-292: We analyzed this from two perspectives: the sample size of outliers and the diversity of outliers.
elements.sent-2411-293: Specifically, we constructed a series of subsets from the \textcolor{deepgray {Imagenet-RC dataset to generate low-quality auxiliary outliers datasets with different sample size and diversity.}}
elements.sent-2411-294: Afterwards, we used these constructed low-quality subsets as the auxiliary outliers dataset to train the model.
elements.sent-2411-295: All experimental results are run over three times and averaged.
elements.sent-2411-296: The experimental details are as follows: Decreasing the sample size of auxiliary outliers.
elements.sent-2411-297: To explore the impact of sample size on our experimental results, we keep the number of classes constant and decrease the size of the auxiliary outliers dataset.
elements.sent-2411-298: This is achieved by applying downsampling techniques, resulting in subsets with the same classes as the original \textcolor{deepgray {Imagenet-RC dataset but with sizes of \textcolor{deepgray {$\{100\%, 85\%, 70\%, 55\%, 40\%, 25\%, 10\%\}$} compared to the original auxiliary outliers dataset.}}}
elements.sent-2411-299: Decreasing the diversity of auxiliary outliers.
elements.sent-2411-300: To investigate the effect of outlier diversity on OOD detection performance, we further reduce the number of classes included in the subset.
elements.sent-2411-301: Specifically, we keep the sample size of the subset at $10\%$ of the original outliers dataset, but gradually decrease the number of classes included (as the number of classes decreases, the number of samples per class increases, ensuring a consistent overall sample size).
elements.sent-2411-302: We constructed a series of subsets with \textcolor{deepgray {$\{1000, 850, 700, 550, 400, 250, 100\}$} classes to serve as auxiliary outliers for experimental evaluation.}
elements.sent-2411-303: In this section, we conduct an ablation study from two perspectives.
elements.sent-2411-304: Firstly, we compare our method with traditional data augmentation techniques (semantic-preserving) to demonstrate that our method effectively enhances the diversity of outliers by altering their semantics.
elements.sent-2411-305: Secondly, considering that our method is an improved variant of mixup, we investigate different mixup strategies to explore what factors contribute to the performance gains.
elements.sent-2411-306: Detailed experimental results are shown in Table ~\ref{tab:ablation .}
elements.sent-2411-307: Ablation study (\uppercase\expandafter{\romannumeral1): Ablation study with different data augmentation method.}
elements.sent-2411-308: To investigate if \textcolor{deepgray {diverseMix offers unique advantages over other data augmentation techniques in enhancing the diversity of outliers, we select different data augmentation methods to process the auxiliary outliers and validate their impact on performance.}}
elements.sent-2411-309: Specifically, we choose semantic-invariant data augmentation methods: \textcolor{deepgray {Gaussian noise \citep{noise , \textcolor{deepgray {cutout \citep{cutout , and \textcolor{deepgray {color jitter for comparison with our method.}}}}}}}}

elements.sent-2411-310: Here, we introduce an appropriate level of noise to the training data to augment its diversity and quantity.
elements.sent-2411-311: We incorporate Gaussian noise with a mean of 0 and a variance of 0.1.
elements.sent-2411-312: To effectively mitigate the risk of model overfitting to Gaussian noise, wherein the model incorrectly classifies any image with Gaussian noise as an OOD input and any noise-free image as an ID sample, this type of noise is applied to only half of the outlier samples during the model training phase.. Cutout.
elements.sent-2411-313: Cutout is a data augmentation technique that introduces random masking of small regions in input images, preventing the model from relying on specific features.
elements.sent-2411-314: In our study, we apply the cutout augmentation to half of the auxiliary outlier samples.
elements.sent-2411-315: This involves randomly masking out small regions within these outlier images by setting all pixel values in the masked regions to zero.. Color jittering.
elements.sent-2411-316: Color jittering is a widely adopted data augmentation technique in image processing.
elements.sent-2411-317: It introduces random variations to the brightness, contrast, saturation, and hue of an image, simulating the diverse conditions encountered in real-world scenarios, such as different lighting environments or camera settings.
elements.sent-2411-318: Specifically, for each auxiliary outlier image, we randomly adjust its brightness within a range of $\pm 0.4$, its contrast within a range of $\pm 0.4$ and its saturation within a range of $\pm 0.4$, while rotating the hue by $\pm 0.1$ radians.
elements.sent-2411-319: This data augmentation strategy preserves the semantic content of the original outlier image while introducing controlled variations in color properties.
elements.sent-2411-320: Ablation study (\uppercase\expandafter{\romannumeral2): Ablation study with different semantic interpolation method.}
elements.sent-2411-321: To explore how diverseMix differs from other mixup-based methods, we compared the performance to \textcolor{deepgray {vanilla mixup and \textcolor{deepgray {cutmix .}}}}
elements.sent-2411-322: We set the hyperparameter $\alpha=4$, consistent with our method \textcolor{deepgray {diverseMix .. Vanilla mixup.}}
elements.sent-2411-323: Here, $\lambda$ is usually sampled from a preset beta distribution $Beta(\alpha,\alpha)$.
elements.sent-2411-324: In this section, we analyze the main hyperparameters involved in our method.
elements.sent-2411-325: The experimental results are shown in the table ~\ref{hyperparameters .}
elements.sent-2411-326: From the experimental results, we find that diverseMix is more effective with larger values of $\alpha$.
elements.sent-2411-327: A larger $\alpha$ means that the model will adopt a more aggressive interpolation strategy, generating mixed outliers that deviates further from the original samples.
elements.sent-2411-329: The temperature $T$ controls diverseMix's sensitivity to the samples, an appropriate $T$ allows diverseMix to accurately perceive the model's familiarity with the samples.
elements.sent-2411-330: In addition, we provide our strategy for hyperparameter adjustment in practice as follows: hyper-parameter tuning.
elements.sent-2411-331: We can first determine the largest possible value of $\omega$ for the original baseline model while maintaining the ID classification accuracy.
elements.sent-2411-332: Then, we can select more suitable parameters for $\alpha$ and $T$, with adjustments made using an OOD validation set distinct from the testing OOD dataset.
elements.sent-2411-333: For example, a subset from the auxiliary outliers could serve as an OOD validation set.
elements.subsection-2411-019: DiverseMix for OOD Detection in Natural Language Processing.
elements.sent-2411-334: To further validate the applicability of our method in non-image domains, we explore the use of diverseMix in the task of Natural Language Processing, following the setting of OE \citep{oe .. Experimental Setting.}
elements.sent-2411-335: We use the \textcolor{deepgray {SST dataset as the ID data, while utilizing the \textcolor{deepgray {WikiText-2 dataset as auxiliary outlier data.}}}}
elements.sent-2411-336: We employ the \textcolor{deepgray {SNLI , \textcolor{deepgray {Multi30K , \textcolor{deepgray {WMT16 , and \textcolor{deepgray {Yelp Reviews datasets as OOD test set.}}}}}}}}
elements.sent-2411-337: We use \textcolor{deepgray {QRNN \citep{lstm language models as baseline OOD detectors.}}}
elements.sent-2411-338: Initially, we train vanilla models for 50 epochs and subsequently fine-tune them on the \textcolor{deepgray {WikiText-2 dataset using \textcolor{deepgray {OE or \textcolor{deepgray {DiverseMix for an additional 5 epochs.}}}}}}
elements.sent-2411-339: Outlier Exposure is implemented by adding the cross entropy to the uniform distribution on tokens from sequences in $\mathcal D_{aux}$ as an additional loss term.
elements.sent-2411-340: For \textcolor{deepgray {DiverseMix , we apply mixup strategy at embedding level, and the loss function is consistent with \textcolor{deepgray {OE .. Experimental Results.}}}}
elements.sent-2411-341: The results presented in table~\ref{nlp highlight that: 1) The incorporation of auxiliary outliers enhances OOD detection performance in non-image domains.}
